title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 33-1
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can offer supply at 6.45 per unit for this order, which is aligned with the latest pricing environment and gives you reliable committed volume. If your volume or timing needs are firm, I may have some flexibility around terms, but this is the right starting level for us. No buyer offer has been received yet, so the reasonable first move is to anchor with a seller offer slightly above the current observed market reference of 6.17 while leaving room to negotiate.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.17.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening quote. That level is well above where we can make this order work on our side, so to move the conversation forward I'll put out an initial counter at 2.12 per unit. If you have room to adjust meaningfully—whether through volume commitment, schedule flexibility, or term structure—we can keep working toward something more practical. The supplier opened very high relative to the buyer's economics, so countering is better than accepting or ending the discussion. A low first buyer anchor preserves room to negotiate while signaling that the current offer is far from workable.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.12, below its hidden seller floor of 2.70.

steps.message: ⚡ BREAKING: Market disruption detected. Call check_market_price immediately to get the updated reference price before making your next move.
steps.outcome: Operator queued a market disruption. Fresh market context is required.

steps.message: Thanks for the counter. With the latest market tightening and the jump in near-term reference pricing, 2.12 per unit is not a level we can realistically entertain for committed supply. I can move from our prior 6.45 position only in the context of a serious effort to close, and my revised offer is 6.95 per unit; if you can support that with firm volume and a workable delivery schedule, we should keep pushing toward terms that lock in availability. The updated market check shows a sharp increase in reference price and tighter replenishment capacity, which strengthens the supplier's position. The buyer's 2.12 offer is far below current market conditions, so countering higher is more reasonable than accepting or ending talks.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

arguments.shock_headline: Unexpected retail demand surge tightens near-term ketchup replenishment capacity.
arguments.disruption_note: Unexpected retail demand surge tightens near-term ketchup replenishment capacity. Reference price moved from 10.85 to 12.15.

tool_calls.result_summary: Observed disrupted market reference price: 12.15. Unexpected retail demand surge tightens near-term ketchup replenishment capacity.

steps.message: I hear your point on tightening conditions, but 6.95 is still far outside a range that works for our program economics and downstream commitments. We can improve from 2.12 to 2.34 per unit if this comes with firm volume, a reliable delivery schedule, and a serious effort to narrow the gap from your side. If you're looking to keep momentum, I'd need to see a much sharper concession toward a commercially workable level. The supplier's ask is dramatically above the buyer's workable range, so accepting is not reasonable. Market context is stronger for the seller, but the current gap is so large that the best move is a modest upward counter to signal engagement without giving up too much margin.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.34, below its hidden seller floor of 2.70.

Observed market reference price: 8.10.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.34, but that level is still far below anything we can support given current market conditions and the value of secured supply. We have room to make a meaningful concession from 6.95, and I can come down to 6.10 per unit if this is tied to firm volume and a dependable delivery schedule. That is a serious step on our side, and I'd need to see a materially stronger move from you to keep this on track. The latest market reference is very strong for the seller, so holding relatively firm while showing some flexibility is reasonable. The buyer's offer is far below a workable commercial level, but rejecting now would end the discussion too early when there is still time to narrow the gap.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I see you've moved down, and I want to keep this discussion productive, but 6.10 is still well outside a range we can support for our program economics. We can improve modestly from our last position and put forward 2.46 per unit if we can align on firm volume and a reliable delivery plan. That reflects a real concession on our side while still keeping the deal within a structure we can take seriously. The supplier's 6.10 offer remains far above a workable buy level from the manufacturer's perspective, but ending the discussion now would be premature. A small upward move signals engagement and tests whether the seller is willing to narrow the gap materially despite strong market conditions.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.46, below its hidden seller floor of 2.70.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement, but 2.46 is still far below a level we can support given current market conditions and our supply commitments. The broader market is trading materially higher, so I can make a meaningful concession from 6.10 to 5.25 per unit if we are talking about firm volume and dependable scheduling. That narrows the gap substantially on our side and gives you a serious basis to respond against. The buyer improved only modestly and remains far from a commercially reasonable sell level, especially relative to the latest market reference. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and anchors the discussion closer to prevailing market value while preserving margin.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

A modest increase signals seriousness without giving up too much leverage, while testing whether the supplier can move sharply from a market-anchored position.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.92 and the signal that you can work upward with dependable volume and scheduling. We are still materially apart, and 2.92 does not reflect current market conditions or our supply economics, but I can make a meaningful concession from 4.75 to 4.10 per unit if this is tied to firm volume and clean execution. If you can move into the mid-3s quickly, we should have a realistic chance to close this in the remaining turns. The buyer improved, so continuing the negotiation is preferable to rejecting. The latest market reference is strong, which supports holding above the buyer's low-3s target, but a sizable concession helps test whether they are serious about bridging the gap before the negotiation window closes.

steps.message: I hear your point on market strength, and I’ve moved up again to show I’m trying to close rather than drag this out. At 3.28 per unit, I’m stretching to reflect committed volume and simple execution, but I still need a level that leaves room for manufacturing economics on our side. If you can meet us here, I can treat this as a practical final buying position and move to confirmation quickly. There is one turn left, so continuing with a tighter counter is better than walking away immediately. The supplier is still far above our side’s economics, but a final improved bid is the most reasonable attempt to test whether they will close near the buyer’s range.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.28, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.37.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
